unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit, your child learns the words for family members (mum, dad, sister, brother, grandma, grandpa), some breakfast and fruit words, and how to count from 1 to 10. They talk about who is in their family and how families help and have fun together. The gentle idea is that families love and help each other. The goal is warm, confident talking in English — not perfect spelling.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The woman who has you as her child and cares for you.
practiceSentences: Samira's mother works at the shop.
Where is your mother?
My mother is in the kitchen.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: m - o - t - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say mother slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the people in a family (mother, father, sister, brother, grandma, grandpa, baby).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name some breakfast foods and fruits (pancakes, cereal, milk, mango, banana, grapes).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Count from 1 to 10, and count backwards from 10 to 1.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer simple questions: “Do you like bananas?” → “Yes, I do.” / “No, I don't.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what their family does together (help, work, play, eat, talk, laugh).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Hear the short ‘a’ sound in words like cat, hat and map.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

readings.title: Breakfast at Grandma's House
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
It was Saturday morning. Amal woke up early. “We are going to Grandma's house!” she said.

Amal has a big family. She has a mother and a father. She has a little brother named Idris. Idris is a baby. He is very small.

“Come, Amal,” said her mum. “Hold my hand.” They walked to Grandma's house together.

Grandma opened the door. “Hello, my dear ones!” she said. She gave everyone a big hug.

“I love you, Grandma,” said Amal.

Grandpa was in the kitchen. “Good morning!” he said. “Who is hungry? It is time to eat!”

“Me! Me!” said Amal. She was very hungry.

On the table there was lots of fruit. There was one mango. There was one yellow banana. There were ten green grapes. There were four red strawberries.

“Let us count the grapes,” said Grandpa. Amal counted: “one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten!” Ten grapes!

“Well done, Amal!” said Grandpa.

“Can I help?” asked Amal. “Yes, please,” said Grandma. “You can lay the table.” Amal put out the little bowls. One, two, three, four, five bowls.

Everyone sat down. They ate the sweet mango. They ate the soft banana. Baby Idris ate his cereal with milk. Some of it went on his nose! Everyone began to laugh.

“Idris is a funny baby,” said Amal, and she gave him a kiss.

After breakfast, the family sat together. Mum and Dad talked with Grandma and Grandpa. Amal helped to tidy the bowls. Then she played a counting game with Idris.

“I love Saturday,” said Amal. “I love my family best of all.”

Grandma smiled. “And your family loves you, little one,” she said.

readings.title: Talk about Family Time
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. This is my ___. He is my ___. She is my ___. Do you like ___? There are ___ ___. I can help by ___. An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: Family Time rhyme
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
The “Families” poem — say it together
Some families are big, / Some families are small. / I love my family best of all.
After you say it, ask your child: “Is our family big or small? Who is in our family?” Say the poem three times — a little louder each time!
The “Ten in the Bed” counting song
There are 10 in the bed / And the little one says, ‘Roll over. Roll over.’ / So they all roll over and 1 falls out… (count down to 1) / There is 1 in the bed / And the little one says, ‘Good night!’
Hold up ten fingers and put one down each time. This is a fun way to practise counting backwards.

comprehension.question: What is the name of Amal's baby brother?
comprehension.explanation: The story says Amal has a little brother named Idris, who is a baby.

comprehension.question: How many green grapes were on Grandma's table?
comprehension.explanation: Amal counts the grapes with Grandpa, one to ten.

comprehension.question: What did Amal do to help before everyone sat down?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She laid the table with five bowls
comprehension.explanation: Grandma says 'You can lay the table,' and Amal puts out five little bowls.

comprehension.question: The mango was sweet. Which word tells us about the banana?
comprehension.explanation: The story says they ate the sweet mango and the soft banana.

comprehension.question: How does Amal feel about her family? Say her words.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She loves them: 'I love my family best of all.'
comprehension.explanation: At the end Amal says, 'I love Saturday. I love my family best of all.'

comprehension.question: Why did everyone laugh at baby Idris?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because his cereal went on his nose
comprehension.explanation: Some of Idris's cereal went on his nose, and everyone began to laugh.

comprehension.question: Which talk line do you use to name a boy in your family?
comprehension.explanation: The line 'He is my ___' is for a boy, such as a brother.

comprehension.question: Finish this talk line: 'I can help by ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: I can help by laying the table. (any true way of helping)
comprehension.explanation: The last talk line is 'I can help by ___.'

comprehension.question: One talk line asks a question. Say that line.
comprehension.explanation: 'Do you like ___?' is the only question in the talk lines.

comprehension.question: Say 'There are ___ ___' about the chairs you can see.
comprehension.correctAnswer: There are four chairs. (any true number)
comprehension.explanation: The talk line 'There are ___ ___' is for counting and naming things.

comprehension.question: Which talk line do you say about a girl in your family?
comprehension.explanation: The line 'She is my ___' is for a girl, such as a sister.

comprehension.question: Point to a family picture and say, 'This is my ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: This is my grandma. (any family member)
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'This is my ___,' for pointing at your family.

grammar.title: Introduce a family member
grammar.explanation: Show a family photo or drawing and say who it is. The word 'my' shows they belong to your family.
grammar.commonMistake: 'This is my' can shrink to 'This my'. Say the little word 'is' clearly in the middle.
grammar.memoryTip: Point at your own heart for 'my', then point at the person.
grammar.practice: Introduce three people: This is my mother. This is my brother. This is my grandmother.

grammar.title: Use 'he' for a boy or a man
grammar.explanation: When you talk about a boy or a man, use the word 'he'. Then say who he is to you.
grammar.commonMistake: Mixing up 'he' and 'she' is very common at this age. Ask gently: is this person a boy or a girl?
grammar.memoryTip: 'He' starts with the same sound as Hassan, a boy's name.
grammar.practice: Talk about three males in your family: He is my father. He is my brother. He is my uncle.

grammar.title: Use 'she' for a girl or a woman
grammar.explanation: For a girl or a woman, use the word 'she'. Say it with a clear /sh/ sound at the front.
grammar.commonMistake: Children may say 'He is my sister.' Listen for the /sh/: girls and women take 'she'.
grammar.memoryTip: 'She' begins with /sh/, like Samira's quiet shhh.
grammar.practice: Talk about three females in your family: She is my mother. She is my sister. She is my aunt.

grammar.title: Ask your family a 'Do you like?' question
grammar.explanation: Ask someone at home about the things they enjoy. Lift your voice up at the end.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'You like dates?' English questions begin with 'Do': 'Do you like dates?'
grammar.memoryTip: Lift your hand upwards at the end, because your voice goes up too.
grammar.practice: Ask three questions at home: Do you like dates? Do you like rice? Do you like tea?

grammar.title: Count the people in your family
grammar.explanation: Count first, then say the number and the thing together. Use this when there is more than one.
grammar.commonMistake: 'There are four child' is a common slip. More than one needs the plural word: 'children'.
grammar.memoryTip: Tap each person gently as you count, then say the whole sentence.
grammar.practice: Count and say three: There are five people. There are two sisters. There are three beds.

grammar.title: Say how you help at home
grammar.explanation: Think of one kind job you do at home. After 'by', the action word ends in -ing.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often say 'I can help by wash.' After 'by' the action needs -ing: 'by washing'.
grammar.memoryTip: Say 'by', then make the action with your hands: washing, sweeping, carrying.
grammar.practice: Name three ways you help: I can help by washing cups. I can help by sweeping. I can help by tidying toys.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Point to people in your family photo as an adult names them, then name them yourself. Say: 'This is my mother.' 'This is my father.' 'This is my baby sister.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern This is my ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: An adult says a sentence about a boy in your family and you copy it. Say: 'He is my brother.' 'He is my grandfather.' 'His name is Yusuf.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern He is my ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask and answer about the girls in your family. Say: 'Who is she?' 'She is my sister.' 'Her name is Samira.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern She is my ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask a friend about the halal food they eat at home. Say: 'Do you like rice?' 'Yes, I like rice.' 'No, I do not like beans.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern Do you like ___? Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Count the people at your table and tell someone. Say: 'There are four people.' 'There are two brothers.' 'There is one baby.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern There are ___ ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Tell the class how you help at home, all on your own. Say: 'I can help by washing the cups.' 'I can help by tidying up.' 'Helping makes Mum happy.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can help by ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw your family sitting together at dinner. Count the people in your picture, then tell an adult who each one is.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'mother' and 'father' twice each. Say the word slowly as you trace, then point to them in a photo.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Match the words brother, sister and baby to the right faces in a picture. Then copy the word for the person closest to your age.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Label two people in your family drawing. Write 'Mum' above one and 'Dad' above the other. Use a capital letter for each name.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy and finish: 'There are ___ ___.' Count first. For example, 'There are three sisters.' Write it twice with different numbers.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write one sentence about helping: 'I can help by ___.' Choose sweeping, washing or carrying. Draw a tiny picture beside your sentence.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Point to each person in your family drawing and name them: 1. mother, 2. father, 3. brother, 4. grandmother. Say 'This is my ___' every time.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw yourself holding a birthday number. Then tell an adult: 'I am ___ years old. My name is ___. This is my family.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the fruit: the mango yellow, the banana yellow, the grapes green and the dates brown. Then say which one your family likes best.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Act out three ways you help at home: sweeping, carrying a bag, folding clothes. An adult guesses each one, then you swap turns.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: Put out five plates and count them aloud: one, two, three, four, five. Then set the table for your family and count again.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make a thank-you card for someone at home. 1. Fold the paper. 2. Draw them. 3. Write their name. Give it and say: 'I can help you.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

assignments.title: My Family Time show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Make a family page. 1) Draw everyone in your family, or make little paper people for them. 2) Label two of them with traced words: mum, dad, sister, brother, grandma or grandpa. 3) Count and write how many: There are ___ people. 4) Record yourself pointing and saying: This is my ___. He is my ___. She is my ___. 5) Grown-ups, write down one way your child helps at home, like laying the table.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which word names a person in your family?
quizzes.explanation: A grandma is a family member; a pencil, rain and a bus are not people at all.

quizzes.question: What do we call your mother's mother?
quizzes.explanation: Your mother's mother is your grandma; a sister is a girl your own age in your family, and a teacher and neighbour are not family.

quizzes.question: Yusuf is a boy in Aisha's family. Which word tells us who he is?
quizzes.explanation: A boy in your family is your brother; sister, mother and grandma are all girls or women.

quizzes.question: Samira is a girl. Which sentence about her is correct?
quizzes.options: They is my sister. | It is my sister. | He is my sister. | She is my sister.
quizzes.explanation: We say she for a girl; he is for a boy, it is for a thing, and they is does not match.

quizzes.question: Which word means the same as mum?
quizzes.explanation: Mum and mother are two words for the same person; a sister, grandpa and brother are different people.

quizzes.question: Aisha shows a photo of her father. Which sentence should she say?
quizzes.options: This is my grandma. | This is my father. | This is my school. | This is my cat.
quizzes.explanation: The photo shows her father, so only this sentence tells the truth about the picture.

quizzes.question: Which word completes the sentence: He ___ my brother.
quizzes.explanation: We say he is for one person; are is for more than one and am only goes with I.

quizzes.question: Which sentence about counting your family is written correctly?
quizzes.options: There are four person in my family. | There is four people in my family. | There four people in my family. | There are four people in my family.
quizzes.correctAnswer: There are four people in my family.
quizzes.explanation: For more than one we say there are and people; there is and person are for only one.

quizzes.question: Which one is a question you can ask your friend?
quizzes.options: Do you like dates? | I like dates. | Dates are nice. | We eat dates.
quizzes.explanation: Only this one asks something and ends with a question mark; the others just tell us things.

quizzes.question: Which word begins with the same sound as sister?
quizzes.explanation: Sister and sun both start with /s/; mat, dog and pen start with /m/, /d/ and /p/.

liveSessions.title: Meet the words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring crayons and one real object connected to the Unit.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hello song; 7 min model and repeat; 8 min game or story; 7 min guided practice; 3 min celebrate and preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Show one word, action, drawing or sentence from Family Time to an adult.

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the people in a family (mother, father, sister, brother, grandma, grandpa, baby).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name some breakfast foods and fruits (pancakes, cereal, milk, mango, banana, grapes).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can count from 1 to 10, and count backwards from 10 to 1.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask and answer simple questions: “Do you like bananas?” → “Yes, I do.” / “No, I don't.”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what their family does together (help, work, play, eat, talk, laugh).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can hear the short ‘a’ sound in words like cat, hat and map.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level3: Labels or copies a meaningful phrase
